Assignment 1: Overview of Visualizations

Salaries and workers in Data Science

Author

Mykola Utkin

Visualization 1 - Grouped Bar Chart

Image:

Source: https://www.kdnuggets.com/data-science-salaries-job-market-analysis-2024-2025

Chart type: Vertical Grouped Bar Chart

Mapping Analysis

What’s being shown Data type Channel used
Job roles (18 positions: Data Scientist, ML Engineer, etc.) Nominal Position (Y-axis), ordered vertically
Experience levels (Entry-Level 0-2 yrs, Mid-Level 3-5 yrs, Senior-Level 5+ yrs) Ordinal Grouped bars with distinct colors (teal, coral/orange, purple)
Salary ranges in USD (minimum and maximum for each level) Quantitative Bar length (horizontal position/X-axis)
Exact salary values Quantitative Text labels at the end of each bar (e.g., “$95,000-$130,000”)

Effectiveness Note

The horizontal orientation effectively accommodates long job titles without text overlap or rotation, making role names immediately readable. The three-color grouping (teal for entry, orange for mid, purple for senior) creates clear visual hierarchy that allows quick comparison both within roles (salary progression) and across roles (which positions pay more at each level).

Visualization 2 - Multi-line Heat Map

Image:

Source: https://www.kdnuggets.com/data-science-salaries-job-market-analysis-2024-2025

Chart type: Multi-line Heat Map Chart (Levels Divided)

Mapping Analysis

What’s being shown Data type Channel used
Job Description (18 position) Nominative Position (Y-axis), line height
Experience level (Entry-Level, Mid-Level, Senior) Ordinal Position (X-axis)
Salary change Quantitative Line slope/angle showing rate of increase, Color hue(from blue to red)
Individual data points Quantitative Numbers on each slot, Color hue (white,black)

Effectiveness Note

The line trajectories illustrate strict dependency between color and value. With senior salaries growing from approximately $120K to $190K while entry-level positions rose more modestly from $65K to $110K.

Visualization 3 - Regular Heatmap

Image:

Source: https://www.refontelearning.com/salary-guide/data-science-salaries-unveiled

Chart type: Heatmap (Matrix visualization)

Mapping Analysis

What’s being shown Data type Channel used
Job roles (18 positions arranged vertically: Data Scientist, ML Engineer, etc.) Nominal Position (Y-axis), organized by role category
Experience levels (Entry-Level, Mid-Level, Senior-Level) Ordinal Position (X-axis), left to right progression
Salary ranges in USD Quantitative Color intensity using gradient (from dark blue to dark red), where more red = higher salary
Exact salary ranges Quantitative Text labels within each cell displaying min-max values (e.g., “$95,000-$130,000”)
Salary hierarchy patterns Quantitative Diagonal gradient from light (bottom-left/entry) to dark (top-right/senior)

Effectiveness Note

The sequential color gradient from blue (representing lower salaries around $65K-$85K for entry-level analysts) to deep red (indicating premium salaries exceeding $290K-$370K for senior CDO positions) creates an intuitive visual encoding where the eye naturally gravitates toward warmer cells representing highest-earning opportunities. One notable insight the heatmap reveals is that some specialized roles (AI Specialist, NLP Engineer) have higher entry-level salaries than generalist mid-level positions (Business Data Analyst), suggesting significant market premium for specialized technical skills regardless of experience.

Visualization 4 - Vertical Bar Chart

Image:

Source: https://www.kaggle.com/code/mohamedmagdy191/data-science-salaries-eda-visualization

Chart type: Vertical Bar Chart

Mapping Analysis

What’s being shown Data type Channel used
Number of positions Quantitative Position (Y-axis), organized by value
Year (2020, 2021, 2022, 2023) Temporal Position (X-axis)

Effectiveness Note

Overall it is a pretty badly made chart. It describes no relation between columns and numbers.

Visualization 5 - Pie Chart

Image:

Source: https://www.kaggle.com/code/mohamedmagdy191/data-science-salaries-eda-visualization

Chart type: Pie Chart with percentage labels

Mapping Analysis

What’s being shown Data type Channel used
Job titles (Data Engineer, Data Scientist,etc.) Nominal Pie slice/sector (angular position around 360° circle)
Percentage of total job postings Quantitative Slice angle and corresponding area (larger angle = higher percentage)
Exact percentage values Quantitative Text labels positioned at the approximate center/midpoint of each slice (e.g., “34.8%”, “28.1%”)
Job categories Nominal Color hue (distinct colors assigned to each job title, though colors appear randomly assigned without semantic meaning)
Visual hierarchy of job prevalence Ordinal Slices arranged in descending order clockwise from largest to smallest

Effectiveness Note

The pie chart immediately communicates market concentration, showing that Data Engineer (34.8%) and Data Scientist (28.1%) together comprise nearly two-thirds (62.9%) of all data science job postings, establishing these as the dominant entry pathways into the field. The descending slice arrangement from largest to smallest creates clear visual hierarchy that guides the eye naturally around the circle. However, the chart suffers from typical pie chart limitations: the smallest slices (those representing 2-4% of jobs) are difficult to distinguish and compare visually despite having percentage labels, as human perception struggles to accurately judge small angular differences.
